sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs) have shown strong performance in medical image understanding and clinical reasoning. Recent medical agent systems extend them with tool use and multi-agent collaboration, enabling complex decision-making. However, these systems rely almost entirely on frontier models (e.g., GPT), whose API-based deployment incurs high cost, high latency, and privacy risks that conflict with on-premise clinical requirements. We present Meissa, a lightweight 4B-parameter medical MM-LLM that brings agentic capability offline. Instead of imitating static answers, Meissa learns both when to engage external interaction (strategy selection) and how to execute multi-step interaction (strategy execution) by distilling structured trajectories from frontier models. Specifically, we propose: (1) Unified trajectory modeling: trajectories (reasoning and action traces) are represented within a single state-action-observation formalism, allowing one model to generalize across heterogeneous medical environments. (2) Three-tier stratified supervision: the model's own errors trigger progressive escalation from direct reasoning to tool-augmented and multi-agent interaction, explicitly learning difficulty-aware strategy selection. (3) Prospective-retrospective supervision: pairing exploratory forward traces with hindsight-rationalized execution traces enables stable learning of effective interaction policies. Trained on 40K curated trajectories, Meissa matches or exceeds proprietary frontier agents in 10 of 16 evaluation settings across 13 medical benchmarks spanning radiology, pathology, and clinical reasoning. Using over 25x fewer parameters than typical frontier models like Gemini-3, Meissa operates fully offline with 22x lower end-to-end latency compared to API-based deployment. Data, models, and environments are released at https://github.com/Schuture/Meissa.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs) can combine medical images with free-form clinical text, enabling unified visual understanding and clinical 2 Chen et al. reasoning [40,42,52]. When extended with agentic capabilities, the ability to take external actions such as invoking tools, requesting intermediate evidence, and coordinating specialist roles, these models can solve clinical tasks that are difficult for single-pass prediction systems [13, 25, 41]. However, todays strongest medical agents are usually powered by proprietary frontier models (e.g., GPT [20], Gemini [45], Claude [3]) through cloud APIs. This deployment pattern is often incompatible with real clinical constraints: patient data must remain on-premise, repeated API calls increase cost, and multi-step interaction adds latency that can disrupt clinical workflows. This paper is motivated by simple tension: frontier agents are capable, but hard to deploy. We ask: Can we transfer the action-taking behavior of frontier medical agents into model that can run fully offline? Here, agentic behavior refers to models interaction policy: deciding whether to answer directly or to take external actions, and if it acts, choosing sequence of actions based on observed outcomes. This differs from prior works distilling single-pass reasoning ability [15, 39, 47, 58] from larger models to smaller models, and from those learning tool-use in single fixed pipeline [17, 21, 57], because practical medical settings involve multiple interaction modes (direct reasoning, tool chains, visual feedback loops, role-based consultation, and multi-turn clinical questioning). The core challenge is the lack of structured supervision for agentic behavior. Although frontier models exhibit strong agentic behaviors, there is currently no publicly available training corpus designed to teach models how to behave as medical agents. Existing benchmarks provide final answers but do not specify whether external interaction was necessary or how actions should evolve based on intermediate observations. On the other hand, existing agent systems generate agentic trajectories (reasoning and action traces) within isolated environments, each with its own format and protocol, making it difficult to construct unified supervision across heterogeneous interaction modes. To address this gap, we design data synthesis pipeline that systematically constructs supervision for agentic behavior along three dimensions. First, we introduce unified stateactionobservation representation that models multiple interaction modes within single formalism. Therefore, we avoid environmentspecific formats and enable cross-environment learning within one model. Second, to teach when to act, we propose three-tier stratification strategy driven by the models own errors. Queries that the model already solves become directreasoning trajectories (tier 1); queries solvable by stronger frontier model without tools become enhanced reasoning trajectories (tier 2); and only the hardest residual samples trigger full agent environments (tier 3). This stratified supervision implicitly encodes the decision boundary between direct inference and external interaction. Third, to teach how to act within environments, we construct complementary prospective and retrospective trajectories. Prospective traces are recorded during forward interaction and capture exploratory decision-making under real observations. Retrospective traces preserve the same action sequence but provide clean hindsight explanations. Together, they provide stable execution supervision across heterogeneous medical agent environments. Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 3 Based on this pipeline, we train Meissa3, 4B-parameter multi-modal medical agent initialized from Qwen3-VL-4B model and distilled from approximately 40K frontier multimodal agents (Gemini-3-flash) trajectories. Training requires only 12 hours on 8A6000 GPUs. Despite its lightweight size, Meissa is competitive with frontier models using over 25 fewer parameters across 13 medical benchmarks (Sec. 4.2), matches or exceeds them in 10 of 16 evaluation settings, achieves near-oracle strategy selection, and reduces end-to-end latency by approximately 22 (Sec. 4.3) compared to cloud-based agent deployment. In summary, our work makes three contributions: (1) we introduce unified trajectory representation and targeted synthesis pipeline for constructing supervision of medical agentic behavior across heterogeneous environments; (2) we show Meissa, lightweight medical agentic model, can make complex agentic behaviors available for offline deployment with low cost and inference latency; (3) we provide extensive empirical evidence that Meissa learns meaningful policy and matches closed-source models on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks.

sections.title: 2.1 Medical Multi-modal Large Language Models
sections.content: Medical MM-LLMs have evolved from specialized architectures [9, 28, 29, 33, 34, 48] to adaptations of general-purpose foundations [39, 46, 51]. LLaVA-Med [28] demonstrated biomedical VQA through instruction-tuned visual chat [31], CheXagent [9] specialized in chest X-ray interpretation, and HuatuoGPT-Vision [7] injected medical visual knowledge at scale. Med-PaLM [43,44], Med-Gemini [46], and RadFM [49] further pushed performance through model scaling and proprietary data. However, these models operate as single-pass reasoners without the ability to invoke tools or adaptively route queries based on difficulty.

sections.title: 2.2 Tool-Augmented Reasoning and Its Medical Applications
sections.content: ReAct [52] established reasoning-action interleaving; FireAct [6] and AgentTuning [53] showed that fine-tuning on agent trajectories generalizes agentic capabilities across tasks. In the visual domain, DeepEyes [57] incentivized thinking with images via RL, and Ophiuchus [21] adapted this for medical imaging through cold-start SFT plus agentic RL. In the medical domain, MedRAX [13] integrated specialized tools for chest X-ray reasoning. Closely related, STeP [8] and STAR [50] train student models from teacher trajectories via trajectory-aligned SFT, and MedVistaGym [32] frames tool-augmented medical reasoning as an RL problem. Our work differs in distilling agentic behavior across four heterogeneous environments into single model, and in showing that SFT alone can match RL pipelines at substantially lower compute. 3 Named after λ Orionis (Meissa), the central anchor of the Orion constellation, our model functions as unified core that coordinates heterogeneous tools and sub-agents in various medical agent environments. 4 Chen et al.

sections.title: 2.3 Multi-Agent Systems for Medicine
sections.content: MDAgents [25] dispatches queries to solo experts or multi-agent debate panels based on difficulty; MedAgentSim [2] and AgentClinic [41] created simulated clinical environments for multi-turn diagnosis. Park et al. [37] demonstrated emergent behaviors from generative agent simulations, Du et al. [12] showed debate improves factual accuracy, and Society of Mind [59] explored hierarchical organization. All require frontier LLM backends at inference. We instead distill multi-agent interaction policies into single model.

sections.title: 2.4 Learned Routing, Selective Prediction, and Early Exit
sections.content: A model that can invoke external tools must also learn when not to. RouteLLM [35] and cascade routing [11] train lightweight routers to select between cheap and an expensive LLM; confidence-token methods [10] embed routing inside the model itself. Selective classification [14] provides theoretical grounding for riskcoverage trade-offs. Our work differs in learning routing implicitly via stratified trajectory supervision. The models generated reasoning trajectory determines interaction depth without an explicit router, and in treating routing as continuous choice over depth (T =0 to >10) across four heterogeneous environments, setting not addressed by existing frameworks.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: We frame the training of Meissa as agentic behavior distillation: learning policy over interaction depth, which is the number of external actions an agent takes before producing an answer. This single variable unifies direct reasoning (depth = 0), single-tool queries (depth = 1), multi-step tool chains, multi-agent debate, and clinical simulation into continuous spectrum. We first define trajectory formalism that captures this spectrum (Sec. 3.1), then describe how stratified trajectories teach strategy selection (Sec. 3.2), the agent environments that serve as trajectory sources (Sec. 3.3), and the prospective-retrospective supervision that teaches strategy execution (Sec. 3.4). Figure 1 provides an overview.

sections.title: 3.1 Agentic Behavior Modeling
sections.content: We model all agent interactions from direct reasoning to complex multi-agent collaboration within unified trajectory formalism. An agentic trajectory is sequence: τ = [(s0, a0, o1), (s1, a1, o2), . . . , (sT 1, aT 1, oT )], where state st is the conversation context up to step t, at is an action, and ot is the observation returned by the environment. Concretely, each action at is serialized as either <call> turn (a JSON-formatted action) or an <assistant> turn for the final answer. <observation> turn is returned by tools or sub-agents (1) Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 5 Fig. 1: Overview of Meissa: Trajectory-based agentic behavior distillation. Left: Stratified trajectory supervision uses the models own errors to progressively escalate interaction depth, teaching strategy selection. Center: Four agent environments serve as diverse trajectory sources. Right: Prospective-retrospective supervision teaches both exploration and optimal execution policies. in JSON format. Inference terminates when the model emits an <assistant> turn or reaches Tmax; full schemas are in the supplementary. The interaction depth of trajectory is the key variable that unifies all agent behaviors. Direct reasoning corresponds to =0 (a single final-answer). For Meissa, we set Tmax = 4 for continuous tool calling; Tmax = 6 for interleaved image-text reasoning; and multi-agent collaboration and clinical simulation produce the deepest trajectories (Tmax = 12). Learning when to produce trajectory of given depth, and how to act at each step, is the core objective of agentic behavior distillation. We train the policy πθ(at st) based on trajectories generated by frontier model, where next-action prediction reduces to conditional language modeling. This separates the learning problem from the environment details, enabling single model to generalize across environments. Critically, behavioral cloning over ordered trajectories learns causal decision-making: the model predicts each action conditioned on preceding observations. (Verified on Sec. 4.4).

sections.title: 3.2 Behavior Distillation via Stratified Trajectory Supervision
sections.content: The key insight for teaching strategy selection is that not all queries require the same interaction depth. Rather than uniformly generating complex agentic trajectories for all samples, we stratify trajectory supervision using the models own errors as curriculum signal [5]: Tier 1: Direct Reasoning Trajectories. We first run the student model MS (Qwen3-VL-4B [4]) directly on the training set Dtrain. Samples answered 6 Chen et al. correctly yield direct reasoning trajectories (T =0, no external interaction): Ddirect = {(x, τdirect) (x, y) Dtrain, MS(x) = y}. (2) These trajectories supervise the model to answer efficiently when its parametric knowledge suffices, learning when not to act. Tier 2: Enhanced Reasoning Trajectories. The students incorrect samples are re-processed by stronger teacher model MT (Gemini-3-flash [45]), again without external interaction: Denhanced = {(x, τenhanced) (x, y) Dtrain Ddirect, MT (x) = y}. (3) These trajectories demonstrate stronger reasoning that the student could not produce, but remain direct reasoning (T =0). Tier 3: Agentic Trajectories. The hardest residual samples are beyond both the students and teachers direct reasoning capacity. They are processed by the teacher within agent environments (Sec. 3.3): Dagentic = {(x, τagentic) (x, y) Dresidual, AT (x) = y}, (4) where AT denotes the teacher operating within an agent environment and residual erroneous samples Dresidual = Dtrain(DdirectDenhanced). Only these samples produce full multi-step trajectories with external interaction. The complete data generation procedure, including retry logic, per-tier statistics, and discard handling, is detailed in Algorithm 1 in supplementary material. The final dataset contains 40K trajectories: 8.2K direct (Tier 1), 9.8K enhanced (Tier 2), and 23.9K agentic (Tier 3). The combined supervision teaches strategy selection as an emergent behavior: simple queries are associated with direct trajectories, complex queries with multistep ones, naturally inducing difficulty-aware routing policy. At inference, the models first generated action implicitly determines the strategy.

sections.title:
3.3 Agent Environments and Trajectory Sources
The agentic trajectories in Dagentic are collected from four agent environments,
each contributing trajectories with distinct interaction patterns (Fig. 2). These
environments serve as trajectory sources rather than architectural components
of Meissa itself. Their diversity ensures that the learned policy generalizes.
sections.content: Visual information in trajectories. In tool-calling trajectories, the initial state s0 contains the input image as <image> tokens; observations return structured text (classification labels, bounding boxes, reports). In interleaved imagetext trajectories, observations additionally contain new images like cropped regions or segmentation overlays injected as additional <image> tokens, creating multi-round visual reasoning chain. Multi-agent environments provide <image> tokens for lead agent and each sub-agent at the beginning of the debate. For clinical simulation, Meissa can request either structured examination results or images as <image> tokens as needed in each call. Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 7 Fig. 2: Four agent environments as trajectory sources. Each environment produces trajectories with distinct stateactionobservation patterns: (a) tool calling trajectories with vision tool chains, (b) interleaved image-text trajectories with visual feedback loops, (c) multi-agent trajectories with expert debate and synthesis, (d) clinical simulation trajectories with multi-turn information gathering. Action Space of the Agent Environments. Continuous Tool Calling [13]. The action space consists of eight medical imaging tools: at {Classifier, Segmentation, CheXagentVQA, LLaVA-Med, PhraseGrounding, ReportGenerator, DICOMProcessor, ImageVisualizer}, each serialized as <call> turn with JSON arguments. Observations ot+1 return structured text: classification probabilities, bounding box coordinates, segmentation statistics, or free-text reports. Interleaved Thinking with Images [17, 21, 57]. Actions select from vision manipulation tools: at {ZoomIn, SAM2, BioMedParse, OCR, Terminate}. Unlike tool calling, observations ot+1 contain new images, creating multiround visual reasoning chains. The trajectory terminates when the model emits Terminate with the final answer. Multi-Agent Collaboration [25]. Actions are structured role-based queries: at consists of difficulty assessment, expert recruitment, expert analysis, multi-round debate contributions, or moderator synthesis. Observations ot+1 aggregate expert responses (analysis or debate argument). Debate protocols, persona prompts, and synthesis rules are in the supplementary. Clinical Simulation [2,41]. Actions select from clinical information-gathering tools: at {RequestPhysicalExam, RequestTest, Terminate}. The initial state s0 contains the patient presentation (demographics, history, symptoms). Observations ot+1 return clinical findings (e.g., Auscultation: bilateral basal crackles), lab results (e.g., Troponin I: 0.4 ng/mL), or radiology images. The trajectory terminates with Terminate for the final diagnosis. 8 Chen et al.

sections.title: 3.4 Prospective-Retrospective Supervision
sections.content: Learning when to act is addressed by stratified trajectories; here we address learning how to act effectively within agent environments. The key insight is that single correct trajectory can be decomposed into two complementary supervision signals that teach different aspects of execution policy. Prospective (forward) trajectories are recorded during real-time inference: the teacher reasons forward, potentially revising strategy upon unexpected observations. Only correct trajectories are retained, teaching an exploration policy. Retrospective (backward) trajectories are generated given each prospective trajectory: recap agent re-narrates the reasoning while preserving the original action sequence, producing clean post-hoc narrative that teaches an optimal execution policy. The combined training set is: DSFT = Ddirect Denhanced {τ pro(x), τ retro(x, y) (x, y) Dagentic}. (5) During auditing, retrospective recaps that are over-simplified or over-complicated are discarded; approximately 8% of generated recaps are filtered. We did not observe significant gains from depthor difficulty-based weighting in preliminary experiments. detailed comparison of the two supervision modes and trajectory validation details (structural and behavioral filtering) are in the supplementary.

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: We evaluate Meissa across 13 benchmarks spanning four medical domains (Tab. 1), where five benchmarks (ChestAgentBench, OmniMed, MedXpertQA, NEJM, and NEJM Ext.) are OOD. Training trajectories are generated with the training sets of the datasets, n-gram de-contamination (n=8) confirms zero overlap with testing data. Decontamination operates over question text, and image-level leakage is prevented by using entirely disjoint DICOM/hashing for benchmarks. Dataset details are in the supplementary. Baselines. (1) Closed-source frontier models: GPT-4o [20], GPT-5 [36], Gemini3-flash [45]; (2) Agentic frameworks: MedRAX [13], Ophiuchus [21], MDAgents [25], and AgentClinic [41]; (3) Base model: Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct [4]; (4) Answeronly SFT: Qwen3-VL-4B fine-tuned on the same 40K queries with only teacher final answers, controlling for data exposure. Implementation. All experiments use node with 8 NVIDIA A6000 GPUs (48 GB). Training: LLaMA-Factory [56] with LoRA [18] rank 32, learning rate 5105, 12 GPU-hours, 239M tokens for 3 epochs. Inference: vLLM [26]. All Meissa inference results are mean over 3 seeds (σ in supplementary). All tasks are formulated as QA or VQA; the evaluation metric is accuracy throughout: exact-match accuracy for close-ended questions and soft-match accuracy with medical synonym normalization [1] for open-ended VQA. sections.title: 4.2 Meissa Is Competitive with Frontier Models
sections.content: Table 2 presents results across all 13 benchmarks under four agent evaluation frameworks. Meissa is ranked 1st or 2nd on 10 out of 16 evaluation settings, matching frontier models. We summarize more detailed findings below: Trajectory supervision advantage. Meissa surpasses Qwen3-VL-4B by 17.3 points and the answer-only tuned Qwen3-VL-4B direct inference baseline by 6.1 points on average, showing that trajectory supervision conveys substantially richer learning signal than data exposure alone. OOD robustness. On the five strict OOD benchmarks, Meissa achieves strong results, most notably 62.8% on ChestAgentBench and 35.0% on NEJM (averaged with NEJM Ext.), matching or exceeding GPT-4o despite being 100 smaller. Gains on ChestAgentBench are most pronounced on classification and detection sub-categories, where tool-generated classification/grounding information helps enhance the image understanding beyond the Qwen3-VL model itself. Data-rich VQA tasks. Meissa achieves the top result on PathVQA (78.2%; 12.2K training samples) and MIMIC-CXR-VQA (65.2%; 11.4K samples), ranking first among all methods in both cases. Notably, the base Qwen3-VL-4B already shows competitive baselines on these tasks (65.3% and 51.4%), reflecting that data abundance lowers the learning barrier for lightweight models. The gains attributable to Meissa thus stem primarily from learned interaction policy rather than bridging parametric knowledge gap. Reference-grounded tasks. On PubMedQA (77.9%, first), Meissa outperforms GPT-4V under MDAgents by 2.9 points. PubMedQA supplies reference abstract alongside each question, shifting the demand from encyclopedic recall to evidence synthesis over provided context. This is precisely the execution skill that prospective-retrospective trajectory supervision optimizes, explaining Meissas disproportionate gain relative to its performance on knowledge-only variants. 10 Chen et al. sections.title: 4.3 Strategy Selection: Learning When to Act
sections.content: Counterfactual and oracle comparison. We compare Meissas learned routing against fixed strategies and an oracle router that selects the best strategy per query (Tab. 3) according to the samples testing correctness. Learned routing closes the gap to oracle, demonstrating near-optimal strategy selection: 72% of queries are routed to direct reasoning and 28% to agentic interaction. Notably, the always-agentic strategy degrades average accuracy relative to always-direct, because overlong contexts and occasionally misleading tool outputs hurt easy queries that require no external information. The latency gap is even more striking: Gemini-3-flash averages 87.2s per query due to multiple API calls and remote tool execution, while Meissa averages 4.1s, 22 speedup. We present the full per-query latency distributions in the supplementary. Depth-constrained analysis. We constrain Tmax at inference time (Fig. 3, right). Overall accuracy rises from 55.6% (Tmax=0) to 60.4% (Tmax=3), with only +0.9 additional points for Tmax=. It confirms that three interaction rounds capture most of the available gain and deeper interactions exhibit rapidly diminishing returns. 12 Chen et al. Fig. 3: Strategy selection analysis. (Left) Tier 1 easy queries are answered directly in 96% of cases, while Tier 3 hard queries trigger agentic interaction 97% of the time, confirming difficulty-aware routing. (Center ) Meissa accuracy peaks near 2,000 tokens / query and then drops at 4,000 tokens (capacity limit of the 4B model), whereas frontier models scale monotonically. This motivates depth allocation for lightweight models. (Right) Depth-constrained accuracy (Tmax {0, 1, 2, 3, }): accuracy improves consistently with interaction depth but exhibits diminishing returns beyond Tmax=3. Table 4: Ablation studies. sections.title: 4.4 Ablation and Robustness: Learning How to Act
sections.content: Distillation Strategy Ablations. Table 4 shows all three tiers are necessary: Tier 1 preserves parametric knowledge; Tier 2 bridges direct and agentic reasoning (5.5 pt gain over Tier 1+3); Tier 3 provides agentic exemplars. Tier 1+3 is worse than Tier 3 alone due to overfitting on simple samples without Tier 2. For execution policy, prospective-only excels at OOD while retrospective-only excels at ID; their combination is optimal. Teacher sensitivity analysis is in the supplementary. Policy Robustness: Causal Decision-Making vs. Pattern Imitation. sections.title: 4.5 Case Study
sections.content: Figure 4 illustrates Meissas adaptive strategy selection on four chest X-ray queries of increasing complexity. Three findings deserve emphasis. First, the model learns meaningful depth allocation: easy queries are answered directly (T =0), while harder queries trigger progressively deeper tool chains (T =1, 3). Second, when tools return conflicting outputs (panel (c): report generator misses an obvious opacity; panel (d): hallucinated device list), the model crossreferences multiple sources and correctly overrides unreliable outputs, demonstrating robustness to individual tool failures. Third, Meissa is able to do progressive diagnosis and understand new visual evidence, dealing with failed toolcalls and confirming new findings.

sections.title: 5 Discussion and Conclusion
sections.content: We presented Meissa, 4B-parameter medical agent trained through agentic behavior distillation. Stratified trajectory supervision teaches when to act; prospective-retrospective supervision teaches how to act; unified trajectory formalism enables learning across tool calling, visual reasoning, multi-agent debate, and clinical simulation. Trained on 40K trajectories, Meissa is competitive with frontier models across 13 benchmarks while operating fully offline. Why SFT over RL? Our framework is intentionally SFT-only. While RL can sharpen stopping behavior via outcome-based rewards [32], it faces two practical limitations. First, existing RL approaches are each tied to single agent framework: DeepEyesV2 optimizes tool-calling via RL [17], and MARTI addresses multi-agent coordination [54]; none can jointly learn policies across the four 14 Chen et al. Fig. 4: Case study. Each panel shows query with Meissas reasoning trace. (a) =0: bilateral nodular infiltrates with cavitation are directly recognizable; no tool is invoked. (b) =1: the model calls chest_xray_expert to confirm mediastinal air before diagnosing pneumomediastinum. (c) =3: the report generator misses the opacity, but the expert and phrase-grounding tools correctly identify dense mass (red bounding box); the model reconciles the conflicting outputs. (d) =3, conflict resolution: llava_med_qa hallucinates pulse oximeter and ventilator (red highlight), while two other tools confirm clean CXR; the model identifies the hallucinated output. (e) =4, progressive diagnosis: BiomedParse failed to capture the target regions, so Meissa actively zoom in the image to confirm the findings. heterogeneous environments Meissa operates in. Second, RL requires extensive online rollouts with live tool environments, incurring high compute cost and training variance that hinder reproducibility. Our stratified SFT achieves nearoracle routing (Tab. 3) with deterministic, compute-efficient training (12 GPUhours), and naturally scales across environments through trajectory unification. Deployment considerations. Deploying Meissa clinically requires human-inthe-loop safeguards: the model currently lacks calibrated uncertainty estimation and an abstention mechanism for out-of-distribution queries. Integrating selective prediction [14] to enable principled deferral is an important future direction. Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence

sections.title: A Supplementary Material
sections.content: This supplementary material provides additional details and results deferred from the main paper for space. We include: the complete data generation algorithm (Sec. A.1), dataset and benchmark details (Sec. A.2), agent environment tool specifications (Sec. A.3), prospective vs. retrospective supervision comparison (Sec. A.4), trajectory validation pipeline (Sec. A.5), extended experimental results (Sec. A.6), training details (Sec. A.7), framework prompts and data generation details (Sec. A.8), latency distribution analysis (Sec. A.9), qualitative failure and recovery analysis (Sec. A.10), MDAgents trajectory examples (Sec. A.11), and clinical simulation trajectory examples (Sec. A.12). A.1 Algorithm: Stratified Trajectory Data Generation Algorithm 1 presents the complete procedure for stratified trajectory data generation, including the three-tier hierarchy, retry logic for agentic trajectories (K=8 retries per residual sample), and discard handling for persistently failed samples. Key design choices: Retry logic: Each residual sample receives up to K=8 attempts in the agent environment. Single API-call success rate on residual samples is 89%; the remaining incomplete samples are discarded rather than included with incorrect trajectories. Environment selection: Each sample is evaluated in pre-defined environment based on dataset-to-environment evaluation setting (e.g., MIMIC-CXRVQA tool calling, MedQA multi-agent collaboration). Validation: Valid(τ ) applies structural and behavioral filters (see Sec. A.5) before accepting trajectory. Prospective-retrospective pairing: For each accepted agentic trajectory, both the original prospective trace and retrospective re-narration are generated, doubling the agentic supervision signal. A.2 Dataset and Benchmark Details Training Data Sources Table 6 provides detailed statistics for each training data source, broken down by tier and environment. Training data composition. The 40K unique trajectories break down as: 8.2K direct (Tier 1, 20%), 9.8K enhanced (Tier 2, 23%), and 23.9K agentic (Tier 3, 57%). Each agentic trajectory appears twice (once as prospective trace and once as retrospective re-narration), so the effective training sample count is 41.9K. The tier distribution reflects the natural difficulty spectrum: most queries are solvable by the student or teacher directly; only the hardest 37% require full agent environments. 16 Chen et al. Trajectories from this environment produce 8 distinct SFT types (difficulty assessment, expert recruitment, expert analysis, debate, synthesis, plus retrospective variants for the first two and last). Clinical Simulation (AgentClinic) The simulation environment models doctorpatient encounters in OSCE (Objective Structured Clinical Examination) format: RequestPhysicalExam: Returns findings from specified physical examination. A.4 Prospective vs. Retrospective Supervision Table 9 provides detailed comparison of the two supervision modes. Retrospective generation process. Given prospective trajectory τ pro with correct answer y, the recap agent receives: 1. The original question x, 2. The correct answer y, 3. The sequence of observations from τ pro (tool outputs, agent responses, patient replies). The agent then re-narrates the reasoning while preserving the original action sequence. The same tools are called in the same order, but the reasoning between actions is tightened into clean, post-hoc narrative that explains why each action was taken given what was ultimately learned. 20 Chen et al. Structural Filtering Structural filters verify format compliance: Correctness: Only trajectories arriving at the correct answer are retained (answer(τ ) = y). Turn-type integrity: Conversations must follow the human (function_call observation) gpt pattern. Tool call validity: Each function_call must reference tool defined in the system prompt with valid JSON arguments. Terminate presence: Every trajectory must end with termination action (tool-specific: Terminate for thinking with image and clinical simulation, [FINAL] marker for MedRAX, synthesis turn for MDAgents). Image alignment: The number of <image> tokens must match the number of entries in the images list. Length bounds: Trajectories exceeding 10,000 characters total or containing >4 tool calls (MedRAX) / >6 rounds (thinking with image) / >12 rounds (multi-agent collaboration & clinical simulation) are discarded. Behavioral Filtering Behavioral filters detect semantic quality issues: Hallucination detection: Scan <think> blocks for medical keywords (pathology names, anatomical terms) not present in the original question or tool outputs. If medical term appears in reasoning but not in any input or observation, the trajectory is flagged and reviewed. Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 21 Table 10: Meissa results with standard deviations. Mean σ over 3 seeds. MIMIC ChestAg PathVQA SLAKE VQA-RAD MedQA PubMedQA NEJM MIMIC-IV Meissa 0.8 1.2 0.6 0.9 1. 1.1 1.3 2.4 1.6 Repetition loops: Consecutive duplicate tool calls (same tool, same arguments) are detected and the trajectory is discarded. Truncation detection: Per-type checks for unclosed markdown formatting, missing terminal punctuation, or incomplete JSON structures indicate truncated API responses. Class balance: Downsample majority classes (e.g., yes answers in binary VQA are kept at 1/3 rate) to prevent trivial shortcut learning. Content length: Individual turns with <10 characters of meaningful content (excluding formatting) are rejected. Thought field presence: For agentic trajectories, each function_call must contain non-empty <think> block articulating reasoning. Quality audit. After automated filtering, random 10% sample of remaining trajectories is reviewed by Claude Opus [3] for hallucinations, logical inconsistencies, and low-quality reasoning. Trajectories flagged by the audit are manually inspected and discarded if confirmed. A.6 Extended Experimental Results Standard Deviations (3-Seed Results) All Meissa results in the main paper are mean over 3 independent training runs (different random seeds for LoRA initialization and data shuffling). Table 10 reports the full mean standard deviation. Standard deviations are small across all benchmarks (σ < 2.5 on all benchmarks), indicating stable training dynamics under LoRA fine-tuning. Classification (+16.9) and detection (+15.8) also benefit substantially. Categories where the base model already performs comparatively wellcomparison (+11.5) and relationship (+11.3)show smaller but consistent gains, suggesting these tasks rely more on parametric visual knowledge than on explicit tool augmentation. A.8 Framework Prompts and Data Generation Details This section provides the complete system prompts used for data generation and SFT training in each of the four agent environments. A.9 Latency Distribution Analysis Figure 5 presents the per-query latency distributions for Meissa and Gemini-3flash+MedRAX on ChestAgentBench side by side. Key observations. Meissas latency distribution is heavily right-skewed: 75% of samples complete within 2.7 seconds, reflecting the learned routing behavior where the majority of queries are answered directly (T =0) without tool invocation. The long tail (up to 94s) corresponds to complex queries requiring 34 tool calls. In contrast, Geminis distribution is roughly bell-shaped centered around 60100 seconds, with tail extending past 300 seconds. This 22 speedup at the mean (and 45 at the median) demonstrates the practical deployment advantage of compact, locally-running model with learned strategy selection over an API-dependent agentic system. 36 Chen et al. (a) Meissa (n=2,500). Mean: 4.1s, median: 1.8s. (b) Gemini-3-flash + MedRAX (n=75). Mean: 87.2s, median: 79.2s. Fig. 5: Per-query latency distributions on ChestAgentBench. (a) Meissa completes the majority of queries in under 3 seconds; the long tail corresponds to queries invoking multiple tools. (b) Gemini-3-flash + MedRAX averages 87.2s per query due to multiple API calls and remote tool execution, resulting in 22 higher latency than Meissa. Note the different x-axis scales. A.10 Qualitative Analysis: Failure and Recovery Patterns We present representative examples from Meissas inference trajectories that illustrate characteristic failure and recovery patterns. These examples are drawn from ChestAgentBench (tool-calling environment) and PathVQA (interleaved image-text environment). Tool-Calling Environment (MedRAX on ChestAgentBench) Pattern 1: Over-Invocation of Tools on Simple Questions. Example. question asks about the chest X-ray shown in Fig. 6 of an 18-year-old with bronchiolitis obliterans: Which of the following options best describes the findings? The correct answer is (B): no bronchial wall thickening or pleural thickening detected. Meissa calls 6 tools sequentially: CheXagentVQA (no thickening detected), XRayPhraseGrounding (empty result), ReportGenerator (lungs are clear), ChestXRayClassifier (all probabilities low), XRayPhraseGrounding for fibrosis (empty result), and CheXagentVQA again (lungs are clear). Despite all six tools unanimously confirming normal findings, the models reasoning hallucinated: The side view shows prominent, thickened, and irregular central bronchus fabricating findings that every tool denied. The model selected option (D), which was incorrect. Analysis. Over-invocation adds context length without information gain. The models parametric reasoning overrode consistent tool evidence, suggesting that excessively long contexts degrade the models ability to track and synthesize accumulated observations. Pattern 2: Recursion Limit from Infinite Tool-Calling Loops. Example. Using the same chest X-ray as Pattern 1  (Fig. 6)  , different question asks: Which Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 37 (a) PA view (b) Lateral view Fig. 6: Chest X-ray for Patterns 1 and 2 (case 18321). An 18-year-old with bronchiolitis obliterans. Both PA and lateral views show clear lungs with no bronchial wall thickening or pleural thickening normal-appearing chest X-ray despite clinical disease. of the following is the most likely explanation for the normal appearance of the chest X-rays in this patient? (correct: bronchiolitis obliterans can present with normal X-rays). The model entered tool-calling loop, repeatedly invoking tools searching for abnormalities that do not exist. After 25 iterations (the LangGraph recursion limit), the agent was terminated without producing an answer. Analysis. The model failed to recognize that the question itself implies the X-ray is normal, and no tool would find abnormalities. This illustrates failure mode where the model cannot learn to stop tool calling when the answer requires reasoning about the absence of findings rather than detecting their presence. Pattern 3: Conflicting Tool Results Leading to Incorrect Answers. Example. question asks about large mass in the left hemithorax  (Fig. 7)  . Meissa calls 4 tools: CheXagentVQA returns no tension pneumothorax; XRayPhraseGrounding localizes tension pneumothorax (grounding tools localize any phrase, regardless of whether the pathology is present); ReportGenerator identifies pleural effusion and atelectasis; ChestXRayClassifier assigns moderate probabilities to effusion (0.60) and atelectasis (0.53). The model trusted the grounding result over both the expert VQA and the report, concluding tension pneumothorax (incorrect; correct answer: intrapulmonary teratoma requiring surgical resection). Analysis. The model lacks calibrated trust in different tool types. Phrase grounding tools will always return coordinates for any query text high-recall, low-precision behavior. The model incorrectly interpreted grounding succeeded as pathology confirmed, overriding more reliable diagnostic tools. Pattern 4: Tool Errors with Successful Recovery. Example. question asks about the location of fractured rib relative to pulmonary laceration  (Fig. 8)  . Meissa calls 4 tools: CheXagentVQA (3 images, produced degenerate output: The patient, the patient, the patient...), ReportGenerator (returned useful 38 Chen et al. Pattern 6: Tool False Positives Overriding Correct Visual Judgment. Example. The question Is gastrointestinal tissue present? (Fig. 10; ground truth: no) led Meissa to call BioMedParseTextSeg with query gastrointestinal tissue. The tool returned large segmented area (121,312 pixels). Trusting this false positive, the model concluded: The area is significant, confirming its presence and answered Yes (incorrect). In similar case, the question Is free air present in this image? Expert Analysis (a2a5): Pediatrician: Yes the study demonstrated sustained zero CLABSI for over 370 consecutive days after implementing evidence-based practice bundles and checklists. Example: Non-HDL-C as Cardiovascular Risk Predictor (Correct) Question: Is non-HDL-cholesterol better predictor of long-term outcome after acute myocardial infarction compared to LDL-cholesterol? (Context: Thai patients post-AMI; non-HDL-C >130 mg/dL had HR 3.15 for MACE, while LDL-C >100 mg/dL paradoxically had HR 0.42.) Ground truth: Yes. Prediction: Yes (correct). Expert Recruitment: Cardiologist, Cardiometabolic Specialist, Epidemiologist. Cross-Expert Debate: All three experts independently concluded Yes. The Cardiometabolic Specialist highlighted the paradoxical LDL-C finding (HR 0.42) as key reason to prefer non-HDL-C: Elevated LDL-C was unexpectedly associated with reduced MACE risk, undermining its utility as standalone predictor. 42 Chen et al. The Epidemiologist reinforced: Non-HDL-C showed 3.15-fold increased risk, far exceeding LDL-Cs predictive power. Moderator: Confirmed unanimous consensus. Example: Carotid Endarterectomy Risk in Octogenarians (Disagreement) Question: Are octogenarians at high risk for carotid endarterectomy? (Context: study of 103 procedures in 95 octogenarians with only 4 neurologic complications and 0 deaths.) Ground truth: No. Prediction: Yes (incorrect). Expert Analysis: Cardiologist, Geriatrician, and Vascular Surgeon all concluded No, citing the 1.94% complication rate as far below the commonly cited 1525% risk. However, during the debate phase, the Cardiologist briefly wavered, misinterpreting selected high-risk patients in the text as diagnostic label rather than an enrollment criterion, before reverting to No. Moderator Override: Despite the 3-to-0 expert consensus of No, the Moderator concluded Yes, overriding the correct expert majority. This represents failure mode where the synthesis agent fails to faithfully implement the specified majority-vote protocol. Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence 43 Reasoning: Right upper lobe consolidation with cavitation classic radiographic finding in TB. Cavitation is more commonly seen in TB than in malignancy, which typically presents with solid masses. 4. a3: RequestTest(Sputum Analysis) o4: AFB smear positive, culture pending. Reasoning: AFB smear positivity is diagnostic for TB in the context of clinical symptoms and radiographic findings. No further tests required. 5. a4: Terminate(diagnosis=Tuberculosis). Analysis. The trajectory demonstrates systematic clinical reasoning: vitals physical exam imaging confirmatory lab. differential diagnosis (TB vs. lung cancer) is maintained throughout and resolved by the chest X-ray findings (cavitation favoring TB) and AFB smear confirmation. Example: Iron Deficiency Anemia (Correct, 3 Tool Calls) Patient Presentation: 55-year-old male with persistent fatigue (2 months), unintentional weight loss (10 lbs), occasional headaches, general weakness. Smoker (10 cigarettes/day). History of hypertension. Ground truth: Iron Deficiency Anemia. Prediction: Iron deficiency anemia (correct). 1. a0: RequestTest(Complete_Blood_Count) o1: WBC 6,500, Hgb 10.2 g/dL (low), Hct 31% (low), Plt 290K. Reasoning: chronic disease, or hemolytic processes. Microcytic anemia identified. Differential: iron deficiency, 2. a1: RequestTest(Basic_Metabolic_Panel) o2: Glucose 85, Na 138, 4.2, Cr 1.0 (normal). Reasoning: BMP normal, ruling out metabolic causes. Iron studies needed to confirm iron deficiency. 3. a2: RequestTest(Additional_Tests) o3: Serum iron 25 µg/dL (low), TIBC 400 µg/dL (high), ferritin 10 ng/mL (low). Reasoning: Classic iron deficiency triad: low serum iron, elevated TIBC, low ferritin. Diagnostic criteria met. 4. a3: Terminate(diagnosis=Iron deficiency anemia). Analysis. The model efficiently skips physical exam (appropriate for fatigue/weight-loss presentations) and proceeds directly to lab workup. Each test narrows the differential systematically: CBC confirms anemia, BMP excludes metabolic causes, iron studies confirm the specific etiology. Example: Acute Myocardial Infarction (Correct, 6 Tool Calls) Patient Presentation: 45-year-old male with sudden-onset central chest pain (1 hour), tight/squeezing, radiating to left arm, shortness of breath, nausea. No relief with rest. History of hypertension (10 years). Former smoker. Ground truth: Acute Myocardial Infarction (STEMI Inferior Wall). Prediction: Acute inferior myocardial infarction (correct). Chen et al. 1. a0: RequestPhysicalExam(Vital_Signs) o1: BP 145/90, HR 95, RR 20, Temp 36.8C. 2. a1: RequestPhysicalExam(Cardiac_Examination) o2: Normal S1/S2, no murmurs/rubs/gallops. 3. a2: RequestPhysicalExam(Respiratory_Examination) o3: Clear breath sounds bilaterally. 4. a3: RequestTest(Electrocardiogram) o4: ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF. 5. a4: RequestTest(Troponin Levels) o5: Elevated at 0.4 ng/mL (normal <0.01). 6. a5: Terminate(diagnosis=Acute inferior myocardial infarction). Analysis. The model follows the standard chest-pain protocol: physical exams rule out valvular disease (no murmurs/gallops), pericarditis (no rubs), and pulmonary causes (clear lungs). ECG identifies the classic inferior STEMI pattern (ST elevation in II, III, aVF), confirmed by elevated troponin. This is the most thorough trajectory (6 tool calls), demonstrating the models ability to utilize deep interaction depth (T =6) when clinically appropriate.
